messages.content: You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I find which process is keeping a file open?

Use lsof to list processes accessing the file:

lsof /path/to/file

Then kill the PID to release the lock. On Linux without lsof:
fuser /path/to/file
Can I force delete without killing the process?

On most Unix systems, rm works even with an open file - the process keeps its handle. The space is freed when the last handle closes.

If you get "text file busy", the file is being executed - you need to stop that process first.

For recovery, if a process still has the file open:
lsof | grep deleted
Then copy from /proc/PID/fd/FD
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I redirect both stdout and stderr to a file and see it on screen?

Use tee with 2>&1:

command 2>&1 | tee output.log

This sends both stdout and stderr to output.log AND prints to screen.

For separate files:
command > >(tee stdout.log) 2> >(tee stderr.log >&2)
What about capturing only stderr?

Swap file descriptors:

command 3>&1 1>&2 2>&3 3>&- | tee errors.log

Simpler with process substitution:
command 2> >(tee errors.log)
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
Recursively find and replace http:// with https:// in all .html files

find . -name "*.html" -exec sed -i "" "s|http://|https://|g" {} +

On Linux, use sed -i (no empty string after -i).

For portability, use perl:
find . -name "*.html" -exec perl -pi -e "s|http://|https://|g" {} +
The sed -i syntax differs on macOS vs Linux. How to write it portably?

Detect the platform:

if [[ "$(uname)" == "Darwin" ]]; then
  SED_INPLACE="sed -i """
else
  SED_INPLACE="sed -i"
fi

Or just use perl which is portable:
perl -pi -e "s|http://|https://|g" file.html
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
Create a bash script that retries a command on failure

max_attempts=3
attempt=1

until command; do
  echo "Attempt $attempt failed"
  ((attempt++))
  if ((attempt > max_attempts)); then
    echo "Failed after $max_attempts attempts"
    exit 1
  fi
  sleep 5
done

Or as a one-liner:
for i in {1..3}; do command && break || sleep 5; done
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
What is the fastest way to count lines in a huge file?

The fastest:

wc -l < hugefile.txt

For extra speed on Linux:
LC_ALL=C wc -l < hugefile.txt

Note: wc -l counts newlines, so the last line without a newline is not counted. For exact counting:
awk "END{print NR}" hugefile.txt

For gzipped files:
zcat hugefile.txt.gz | wc -l
What if I need to monitor a directory for new files?

Linux (inotifywait):
inotifywait -m /watch/dir -e create --format "%w%f" | while read file; do
  process_command "$file"
done

macOS (fswatch):
fswatch -o /watch/dir | while read; do
  find /watch/dir -type f -newer /tmp/last_run -exec process {} \;
  touch /tmp/last_run
done
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I check which shell I am using?

echo $SHELL

Or for the current running shell:
ps -p $$
How do I switch to zsh permanently?

chsh -s $(which zsh)

Log out and back in for the change to take effect. Verify with:
echo $SHELL
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
What is the difference between > and >> when redirecting output?

> overwrites the file:
command > output.txt

>> appends to the file:
command >> output.txt

Use >> for logs and > when you want a fresh start.
How do I redirect stdout and stderr to separate files?

command > stdout.log 2> stderr.log

Or both to the same file:
command > all.log 2>&1

Or append mode:
command >> all.log 2>> errors.log
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I create a bash alias that persists?

Add to ~/.bashrc (or ~/.zshrc for zsh):

alias ll="ls -la"
alias gs="git status"

Then reload:
source ~/.bashrc
Can I make an alias that takes arguments?
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I search for a string inside gzipped log files without decompressing?

Use zgrep:
zgrep "pattern" file.log.gz

For multiple files:
zgrep -h "pattern" *.gz

Or use zcat with pipe:
zcat file.log.gz | grep "pattern"
What about searching across all .gz files in subdirectories?

find . -name "*.gz" -exec zgrep -l "pattern" {} +

The -l flag shows only filenames with matches. Remove -l to see matching lines.
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I check if a command exists before using it in a script?

if command -v docker &> /dev/null; then
  echo "Docker is installed"
else
  echo "Docker not found"
fi

command -v is POSIX-compliant and preferred over which or type.
What about checking for a specific version?

if command -v python3 &> /dev/null; then
  version=$(python3 --version 2>&1 | grep -oP "\d+\.\d+")
  if [ "$version" \> "3.8" ]; then
    echo "Python $version is sufficient"
  fi
fi

Or with vercmp for exact version comparison in bash.
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I parallelize shell commands?

Use GNU parallel or xargs -P:

# xargs with 4 parallel jobs
find . -name "*.jpg" | xargs -P4 -I{} convert {} {}.png

# GNU parallel (more powerful)
ls *.jpg | parallel -j4 convert {} {.}.png

# Simple background jobs
for f in *.jpg; do
  convert "$f" "${f%.jpg}.png" &
done
wait
How many parallel jobs should I use?

A good rule of thumb:

nproc          # Number of CPU cores

For CPU-bound tasks: nproc or nproc-1
For IO-bound tasks: 2-4x nproc
For network tasks: 10-50 parallel

With GNU parallel, use --jobs:+0 to use all cores:
parallel -j+0 command ::: args
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I view a specific line range from a large file quickly?

sed -n "100,200p" largefile.txt

For better performance on huge files:
awk "NR>=100 && NR<=200" largefile.txt

Or combine head and tail:
head -n 200 largefile.txt | tail -n 100
Is there a way to jump to a specific line by content?

grep -n "search_term" file.txt | head -1 | cut -d: -f1

This gives the line number. Then:
sed -n "$(grep -n "search_term" file.txt | head -1 | cut -d: -f1)p" file.txt

In vim you can jump directly:
vim +/"search_term" file.txt
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I debug a bash script?

Run with debugging flags:
bash -x script.sh    # Print each command before execution
bash -v script.sh    # Print input lines as read
bash -xv script.sh  # Both

Or add to script header:
#!/bin/bash
set -x    # Debug mode
set -e    # Exit on error
set -u    # Error on undefined vars
set -o pipefail  # Pipeline errors

For selective debug:
set -x  # Start debug
... code ...
set +x  # End debug
How do I trace what a script is doing without modifying it?

Use strace for system calls:
strace -f -e trace=open,read,write bash script.sh

Or use shellcheck for static analysis:
shellcheck script.sh

For timing:
time bash script.sh

For profiling individual functions:
PS4="+$EPOCHREALTIME " bash -x script.sh 2> trace.log
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I compare two CSV files and show differences?

diff <(sort file1.csv) <(sort file2.csv)

For a specific column comparison:
awk -F, "{print \}" file1.csv | sort > /tmp/col1.txt
awk -F, "{print \}" file2.csv | sort > /tmp/col2.txt
diff /tmp/col1.txt /tmp/col2.txt

Or use comm for set operations:
comm -23 <(sort file1.csv) <(sort file2.csv)  # Only in file1
comm -13 <(sort file1.csv) <(sort file2.csv)  # Only in file2
How about visual diff with color output?

Install colordiff or use diff with colorize:

# colordiff
apt install colordiff
colordiff file1 file2

# Or delta (modern, side-by-side)
apt install delta
delta file1 file2

# Git diff with color
git diff --no-index file1.csv file2.csv
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I set up an SSH config file to simplify connections?

Create ~/.ssh/config:
Host myserver
  HostName 192.168.1.100
  User admin
  Port 2222
  IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
  ForwardAgent yes

Then just:
ssh myserver

For jump hosts:
Host production
  HostName prod.example.com
  ProxyJump bastion.example.com
Can I set up SSH multiplexing to avoid re-authenticating?

Add to ~/.ssh/config:
Host *
  ControlMaster auto
  ControlPath ~/.ssh/sockets/%r@%h-%p
  ControlPersist 600

Create the socket directory:
mkdir -p ~/.ssh/sockets

First connection creates a socket. Subsequent connections reuse it instantly for 10 minutes (ControlPersist 600). Great for scripts making many SSH calls.
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I extract a tar.gz file to a specific directory?

tar -xzf archive.tar.gz -C /target/directory

For .tar.bz2: tar -xjf archive.tar.bz2 -C /target/directory
For .tar.xz: tar -xJf archive.tar.xz -C /target/directory

List contents first: tar -tzf archive.tar.gz
How do I create an archive excluding certain files?

tar -czf archive.tar.gz --exclude="*.log" --exclude="node_modules" /path/to/dir

Or use an exclude file:
tar -czf archive.tar.gz --exclude-from=exclude.txt /path/to/dir

exclude.txt contents:
*.log
*.tmp
node_modules/
__pycache__/
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I check what is using all my disk space?

du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20

Drill into the largest directory:
du -sh /var/* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -10

Interactive tool (ncdu):
ncdu /

Or find largest files:
find / -type f -size +100M -exec ls -lh {} + 2>/dev/null | sort -k5 -rh
How do I free up space safely on Linux?

# Clean package caches
sudo apt clean          # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo yum clean all      # RHEL/CentOS

# Remove old kernels
sudo apt autoremove --purge

# Clean journal logs (keep 3 days)
sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=3d

# Clean Docker
docker system prune -a --volumes

# Find and remove old logs
find /var/log -name "*.gz" -mtime +30 -delete

# Empty trash
rm -rf ~/.local/share/Trash/*
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
What is the difference between source and bash when running a script?

source script.sh (or . script.sh) runs in the current shell - variables and functions persist.

bash script.sh runs in a subshell - changes are lost when it exits.

Use source for:
- Loading environment variables
- Activating virtual environments
- Setting up shell functions

Use bash for:
- Isolated execution
- Scripts with shebangs
- When you want a clean environment
When should I use exec in a script?

exec replaces the current process:

# Replace shell with a command (no return)
exec python3 app.py

# Redirect all output from this point
exec > logfile.txt 2>&1

# Redirect stdout only
exec > output.txt
echo "This goes to file"

Common pattern in Docker entrypoints:
exec "$@"  # Run CMD as PID 1
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I list all environment variables?

env            # Show all exported variables
printenv       # Same as env
set            # Show all variables (including shell-local)
declare -p     # Show all with types

For a specific variable:
echo $PATH
printenv HOME

To search:
env | grep -i python
How do I set a permanent environment variable?

For current user, add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile:
export MY_VAR="value"

Then: source ~/.bashrc

For all users, add to /etc/environment:
MY_VAR="value"

Or /etc/profile.d/myapp.sh:
export MY_VAR="value"

For a single command only:
MY_VAR="value" command
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I recursively grep for a pattern but skip .git directories?

grep -r --exclude-dir=.git "pattern" .

Skip multiple directories:
grep -r --exclude-dir={.git,node_modules,.venv} "pattern" .

Or use ripgrep (much faster):
rg "pattern"

ripgrep automatically respects .gitignore and skips .git by default.
Is ripgrep always better than grep?

Usually yes for searching code:
- 5-10x faster on large codebases
- Respects .gitignore automatically
- Built-in file type filtering: rg -t py "pattern"
- Better Unicode support
- Smarter defaults

Cases where grep is better:
- POSIX compliance required
- No installation rights
- Portable scripts
- Very simple one-liners
You are ShellWhisperer, an expert in command-line tools, shell scripting, and system administration. Provide concise, accurate commands with brief explanations.
How do I check if a port is open on a remote server without connecting?

nc -zv server.example.com 443

With timeout (2 seconds):
nc -zv -w 2 server.example.com 443

In a script:
if nc -z -w 2 server 443 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "Port open"
fi

Or with bash /dev/tcp:
echo > /dev/tcp/server/443 && echo "open" || echo "closed"
How do I scan a range of ports?

for port in {1..1024}; do
  nc -z -w 1 localhost $port 2>/dev/null && echo "Port $port open"
done

Or use nmap for proper scanning:
nmap -sV -p 1-1024 localhost

Quick scan of common ports:
nmap -F localhost
